name: Hoist 128x128 Hot-Band Shared Offsets Out Of The Steady-State Loop

summary.name: Hoist 128x128 Hot-Band Shared Offsets Out Of The Steady-State Loop
summary.hypothesis: The regular 128x128 sibling already showed that a small register reduction and a cleaner consume path can recover some of the grouped-row loss. The next cheapest win is to hoist warp-local A-row and B-column shared offsets out of the hot-band steady-state loops so the active 128x128 path spends fewer issue slots on invariant pointer math while keeping the same CTA geometry, stage depth, and shared-memory footprint.
summary.expected_bottleneck: Warp-local shared-pointer arithmetic and loop-carried control overhead in the 128x128 hot-band steady state are still diluting tensor issue on a latency-limited path.
summary.risk: Low. The change stays inside the active 128x128 hot-band loops and should not alter CTA geometry, staging depth, or ownership.
ranking_notes: This is the cleanest follow-on after the regular-sibling experiment because it preserves the same recovered register footprint and tests whether control overhead is the remaining gap.
It also matches the user request to inject a materially different family without reopening a stale negative branch immediately.
This is the least aligned with the new 256x128 tiling preference, but it is still the cheapest next exploit on the current live 128x128 surface.
summary.stop_condition: Stop if the implementation needs to change CTA geometry, stage count, or dispatch order instead of remaining a local offset-hoist cleanup.
expected_local_changes: Precompute warp-local A and B shared offsets once per warp before the 128x128 steady-state loops.
Keep the current single-K staging and grouped_rows=4 traversal unchanged.

notes: Node C may now implement this one candidate.
